name: Ivory Coast
experiences: Basilica of Our Lady of Peace
avoid: April to October brings the wettest roads and the most humidity, which slows western drives to Man and makes mountain trails around Tonkoui far muddier.
Intercity travel is mostly by bush taxi, so keep daylight buffers for Grand-Bassam, Yamoussoukro, and Man connections instead of stacking tight same-day plans.
Cards work best in larger hotels; keep small CFA cash ready for gbakas, market snacks, local guides, and park gates.
landmark: Basilica of Our Lady of Peace
stopoverNote: Abidjan (ABJ) is the practical gateway for almost every Côte d'Ivoire itinerary. Use gbakas for cheap city hops, bush taxis for most overland legs, and check Air Côte d'Ivoire as a time-saving backup if a domestic schedule lines up with your dates.

cities.name: Abidjan
cities.notes: Lagoon-side high-rises, serious food, Banco rainforest, big markets, and the country's easiest base for onward transport.

cities.name: Yamoussoukro
cities.notes: Monumental basilica, broad ceremonial boulevards, crocodile lake viewpoints, and Côte d'Ivoire's political capital stop.

cities.name: Grand-Bassam
cities.notes: UNESCO-listed colonial quarter, surfy beaches, costume museum, and the easiest heritage side trip from Abidjan.

cities.name: Man
cities.notes: Waterfalls, mountain viewpoints, western market energy, and the jumping-off point for Mount Tonkoui and deeper forest detours.

cities.name: Assinie
cities.notes: Lagoon-meets-ocean scenery, beach lodges, boat excursions, and Côte d'Ivoire's classic easy seaside reset.

itinerary.sim: Orange Côte d'Ivoire is the safest single-network pick for Abidjan, the coast, Yamoussoukro, and western road sections; expect roughly ₹560–₹1,400 for a starter SIM or eSIM bundle with enough data for maps, calls, and messaging.

days.theme: Start with Banco's urban rainforest, Plateau's skyline, and the most polished first-night food options in the country.

days.theme: Keep it slow with a second boat run, a long seafood lunch, and one full dry-season beach day.

days.theme: Aim inland for monumental architecture, broad capital avenues, and the city's signature basilica visit.

days.theme: Push west into the highlands for waterfalls, market energy, and the best cool-weather break in the country.

days.theme: Only add this if you want a serious wildlife extension: Taï is a long southwest detour, but it is the signature rainforest add-on.

activities.name: Early bush taxi or private 4x4 transfer toward Taï or Guiglo sector
Taï National Park foreign visitor entry
Mandatory local eco-guide or tracker
Packed lunch and water for the forest day
Return transport or onward overnight toward the southwest

connections.method: Shared taxi, minibus, or a cheap suburban-style transfer is easiest; this is also the simplest place to sample the local gbaka rhythm before longer legs
Bush taxis and shared cars are the standard coastal move east, usually via Bonoua and Aboisso
This is a longer inland transfer, usually stitched together by bush taxi via Aboisso or Abidjan before continuing to the capital
Bush taxis and coaches are the default westbound option; start early because the road day is long even in dry season
Road returns by bush taxi or coach are the norm, while Air Côte d'Ivoire is worth checking only as an occasional time-saving domestic backup if schedules line up

extras: November to March is the cleanest all-round window: Abidjan stays more manageable, Grand-Bassam and Assinie are less stormy, and Mount Tonkoui trails are much less slippery.
Banco National Park and the Yamoussoukro basilica both reward early starts, before midday heat and traffic make city movement slower.
Grand-Bassam's UNESCO quarter itself is free to wander; the paid museum stop is the best-value way to add context without booking a full private tour.
Keep small cash for gbakas, bush taxis, and park gates, because card acceptance drops sharply once you leave Abidjan's bigger hotels.
Man is the most weather-sensitive leg here: if rain is building in the west, cut the Taï extension first and protect the Tonkoui morning instead.
Assinie is best used as a dry-season decompression stop, not a packed sightseeing day, so one boat ride plus a long seafood lunch usually feels right.
